title: The Macro Minute: Is the Fed done with K-shaped monetary policy?

Happy Wednesday out there team42. It's
your skipper here Darius Dell to present
our macro minute for Wednesday July 15th
2026. As always we'll start with the
executive summary from today's leadoff
morning note. So let's dive right in.
Today's key macro question is is the Fed
done with K-shaped monetary policy. The
short answer is no. But Chair Worsh's
three-hour House Financial Services
Committee testimony confirmed this
outcome remains a key structural
downside risk to asset prices,
particularly the prices of gold and
Bitcoin and to a lesser degree stocks.
It remains a key structural upside risk
to Treasury bond prices. In terms of
analytical nuance here, the most
important things we learned regarding
the evolving structural outlook for US
monetary policy in Chair Wars's three
hours of testimony yesterday presented
an order of appearance with respect to
the corresponding comments from the
chair. Number one, the days of the Fed
looking through perhaps blaming supply
shocks for persistent failure to achieve
its price stability mandate are likely
in the rearview mirror. Number two, the
Fed's balance sheet will likely
transition from policymakers largely
relying upon quote financial stability
concerns to provide undue income support
for households and businesses on the top
part of the K-shaped US economy to being
carefully considered in the context of
its price stability mandate. Number
three, consistent with our basian prior,
a significant and protracted decline in
the Fed balance sheet will will not
occur for several quarters, perhaps
longer, uh given the historical timeline
for changes to banking sector uh
regulation. Uh number four, uh Kevin
Worsh will seek to inject the lessons of
humility and forward-looking data
dependence that he learned from one of
the all-time great investors, Stan Duck
Miller, one of my favorite investors, uh
into the Fed's reaction function. Number
five, consistent with our basian prior,
the Fed may seek to deregulate US
commercial banks in a way that passes
the hot potato of marketable treasury
supply exclusively to large banks, which
typically and overwhelmingly serve large
customers. This will allow the Fed to
focus incremental deregulation efforts
on small banks, which would accelerate
the credit impulse in the real economy,
according to the seminal research of Dr.
Richard Warner. Obviously, we got a lot
going on here today, so let me just skip
to the uh to the end here. Uh uh going
to number 14. Uh the Fed may be
embarking on a long and arduous journey
to rid the US economy of K-shaped
monetary policy. If so, achieving this
objective will be challenging and
require at least the next several years
to demonstrate sufficient results
because of all the entrenched group
think within and around the institution.
This is why we are such big fans of
War's task force initiative. The chair
is seeking to use the power of framing
and inception to speed up this critical
process. History has proven extremely
unkind to societies that share our
K-shaped dynamics. Pivoting the Fed away
from K-shaped monetary policy will go a
long way towards shutting off what Dr.
Peter Turchin and his colleagues at
Crisis DB describe as a wealth pump
throughout their seminal research. All
told, we are rooting for Chair Walsh and
his colleagues at the Fed to make us
wrong on our near three-year-old asset
allocation view that gold is a far
better portfolio diversifier than
Treasury bonds. Moreover, we will
happily serve the Fed in any capacity if
we can help the institution affect the
pivot from gold back to bonds as the
primary portfolio diversifier in KISS.
Why? Because a protracted plunge in the
price of gold would be representative of
the best possible monetary policy
outcome for our ailing K-shaped society.
Historic opportunities like this require
both wishful thinking and skilled risk
management. Best of luck to you chair
wars. So as always a wrap- up with the
question from our community. This was
titled will we ever have a recession
again? It feels like the federal
government and federal reserve would
just print if we ever start moving
towards a recession. Thus, we moved to
paradigm D, Printface, uh, which we
haven't started that already. If we
haven't started that already, which I
believe we we have tiptoed into paradigm
D with reserve management purchases
since 2009. It is likely that they made
the decision to never let a recession
happen again. 17 years is the longest
business cycle that I'm aware of without
a recession as I'm not counting the co
the temporary decline in GDP during uh,
COVID. Is my thinking crazy? Uh, and um,
and the the follow-up question here from
another members. Uh, good question. So
if so is inflation the new recession. So
this is the risk in our opinion based on
our all the the hundreds of slides of
research we pump out every every every
week and and probably perhaps thousands
of slides of research we pump out uh to
institutional investors all around the
world uh re investors all around the
world uh every month. Our institutional
clients don't see anything different
from our retail clients. Uh
research which I surmised is probably
the most amount of research that any
single human being is willing to produce
uh on global Wall Street. uh based on
all that research the we are still very
much in um the geopolitically driven
supply demand imbalance in the treasury
bond market. In fact to the tick data
that we got yesterday confirms that that
is progressing in an adverse manner uh
for the perspective of the treasury uh
bond market. But I digress. Uh so as
long as that pred condition persists you
know the the the debt disease which is
paradigm A then there really historic
history has proven there's only three
options for sovereigns to deal with
paradigm A which is you can cut paradigm
B uh you can grow paradigm C or you can
print paradigm D. Uh we right now we're
in paradigm C. We noticed the
administration's pivot from paradigm B
to paradigm uh C uh uh last April when
we authored the 42 macro uh paradigm C
bull market theme uh which obviously
we've all been benefiting from uh since
then. Uh but you know you know looking
into the future and and then some tying
this back to to Worsh's testimony today.
Worse doesn't seem to be on on board
with this at all. And and I've long
contended that you know it doesn't
matter who's the Fed chair. You can put
Paul Vker into this situation and it
won't really matter long term because
you know the the supply demand imbalance
the drivers of the supply demand
imbalance are too great for any Fed
chair to resist and ignore uh despite
whatever their political objectives are.
You know you're they're Fed's going to
get dragged kicking and screaming into
paradigm D uh uh based on our current
projections. Now the risk is is that you
know they try to stave off paradigm D uh
by you know clever uh deregulation of
the banking sector as I highlighted uh
uh earlier in this macro minute uh which
would ultimately you know allow paradigm
C to persist for longer and obviously if
you layer on a productivity boom from AI
which is you know you know clearly a
right tail risk from an economic
standpoint as well as a market
standpoint uh may not be true but it's
certainly a tail risk that we agree If
you layer on a productivity boom to a
bottomupleled
credit expansion, you could have
paradigm C persist for for years in a I
wouldn't necessarily say
non-inflationary manner, but certainly
in a manner uh that that that that
allows you know the the sovereign to to
to to to
improve the disequilibrium that is
occurring right now on the long end of
the tertiary bond curve. the
disequilibrium that you know we called
out back in the summer of 2023
alongside Janet Yell and pivoting to
Duffish net financing policy which you
know our former our fellow Yelli uh
Treasury Secretary now Treasury
Secretary Scott Best and then former
client was saying um you know he was
lambasting it at the time and ultimately
once he got in the seat he uh rubber
stamped the policy which in our opinion
was a big uh clear indication that there
is a supply demand imbalance in the
Treasury bond market uh you know and and
and ultimately we've seen the Fed you
know lower interest rates with inflation
well above target for for years now. And
so ultimately in our opinion we think
that that paradigm a this debt disease
and and and the supply demand imbalance
that that is contributing the debt
disease is contributing to is not the
only reason. Uh there's the geopolitical
reasons uh uh primarily as well as the
the the marketable treasury debt market
composition reasons are contribut to
that as well. Uh as long as that
persists then it's highly likely that
inflation is the new uh recession.
However, based on what we learned from
Cameron Worsh today is that he might do
everything he can in his power to stave
off that dynamic uh in a way that, you
know, sort of reduces the K-shaped uh uh
uh impact of Fed, you know, of Fed
monetary policy that we've seen uh in
recent decades. The Fed getting easier
and easier, progressively easier and
historically easy with respect to its uh
its interest rate policy, you know,
certainly relative to the Taylor rule
and and the balance sheet policy
relative to common sense. So, we'll wrap
it up there. Derry St here presenting
our macro minute for Wednesday, July
15th, 2026. Uh, best of luck out there
today. Like I said, this is a historic
moment. This is a historic moment uh in
asset markets as as indicated by the
historic declines in the price of gold
and silver that we observed I want to
say on July on January 30th when Kevin
Worsh's name uh was first banded about
as as the as the president's nominee for
Fed chair. uh in our opinion that might
have been a historic signal that you
know the day the Fed's days of K-shaped
monetary policy are are done if not you
know delayed for an extended period of
time and so I don't know that we're
going to find the answers to to to this
critical question in asset markets
anytime soon but you know this is why
you have to have you separate your
research from your risk management you
know our research is you know we are in
a supply demand and balance of treasury
bond market the Fed's going to
eventually be forced to print to support
that the risk management is we've been
out of K we've been out of Bitcoin we've
been out of hold in KISS because the
risk management overlays the top down
and bottom up risk management overlays
the ball targeting and ambos position
sizing have helped us you know mitigate
risk in our portfolios and as long as we
can keep our research separate from our
risk management we don't have to uh you
know the burden of proof on being right
on such a big thesis uh it mitigates
that burden of proof in a way that
protects our portfolios and keeps us you
know marching towards comfortably

and so uh we're really proud about that
so we'll wrap it up there catch you back
